
## Broadcasting

The variants discussed below are illustrative examples.

Variant 1 — scalar-array — performing an operation on every element of the array

When a scalar is combined with an array, NumPy broadcasts the scalar to match the array's shape, then performs the operation element-wise. In the code above, array `a` has shape 3×2 and contains [[1, 2], [5, 6], [9, 10]]. The expression `a + 4` conceptually expands the scalar 4 into a 3×2 array filled with 4s, so the result `b` is [[5, 6], [9, 10], [13, 14]]. The same principle applies to `2 ** a`, where the scalar 2 is broadcast across every element of `a`, producing [[2, 4], [32, 64], [512, 1024]] — each element is computed as 2 raised to the corresponding value in `a`.

```{python}
#| echo: true
import numpy as np

a = np.array([[1, 2], [5, 6], [9, 10]])
b = a + 4
print(b)
c = 2 ** a
print(c)

```



Variant 2 — two arrays — "when one of the arrays can be expanded" (both dimensions are equal or one of them is equal to 1)

When two arrays of different but compatible shapes are combined, NumPy stretches the smaller one to match the larger. Array `a` has shape 2×2 and `b` is a 1D array of shape (2,), which NumPy treats as a single row. In `a + b`, the row [9, 2] is broadcast across both rows of `a`, giving [[10, 4], [14, 8]]. Similarly, `a / b` divides each row of `a` element-wise by [9, 2], producing [[1/9, 1], [5/9, 3]]. Array `c` has shape 2×1, meaning it acts as a column. In `a + c`, the value 4 is added across the entire first row and -2 across the second, yielding [[5, 6], [3, 4]]. Likewise, `c / a` divides the column values by each element in the corresponding row, so the first row becomes [4/1, 4/2] and the second [-2/5, -2/6]. The general rule is that dimensions are compatible when they are equal or one of them is 1 — the dimension of size 1 is then stretched to match the other.

::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}

<https://numpy.org/doc/stable/user/basics.broadcasting.html>

:::
  
```{python}
#| echo: true
import numpy as np

a = np.array([[1, 2], [5, 6]])
b = np.array([9, 2])
r1 = a + b
print(r1)
r2 = a / b
print(r2)
c = np.array([[4], [-2]])
r3 = a + c
print(r3)
r4 = c / a
print(r4)

```



Variant 3 — "column" and "row"

Array `a` is created as a row [[5, 2, -3]] and then transposed with `.T`, turning it into a column of shape 3×1. Array `b` is a 1D array of shape (5,), treated by NumPy as a single row. When these two are combined, broadcasting stretches the column across 5 columns and the row across 3 rows, producing a 3×5 result in every case. In `a + b`, each value from the column [5, 2, -3] is added to every element of the row [3, -2, 1, 2, 4], giving [[8, 3, 6, 7, 9], [5, 0, 3, 4, 6], [0, -5, -2, -1, 1]]. The expression `b + a` yields the same result, since addition is commutative. For `a * b`, each column value is multiplied by every row value, producing [[15, -10, 5, 10, 20], [6, -4, 2, 4, 8], [-9, 6, -3, -6, -12]]. This column-times-row pattern is essentially an outer operation — every pair of elements from the two arrays is combined, and the result shape is determined by the lengths of the column and the row.


```{python}
#| echo: true
import numpy as np

a = np.array([[5, 2, -3]]).T
b = np.array([3, -2, 1, 2, 4])
print(a+b)
print(b+a)
print(a*b)
```
